question: When 'CAT' is on a sign, is the word on the sign the same word as 'CAT' on a different sign? In what sense yes, in what sense no?
english_query: When 'CAT' is on a sign, is the word on the sign the same word as 'CAT' on a different sign? In what sense yes, in what sense no?
ugf_response: First, when we put the letters C A T on a thing that shows words, the thing shows a group of marks that look the same as the group of marks that would be shown on any other thing that shows words. In this way the word on the first thing is the same as the word on the second thing, because the same marks are used and they look the same. But at the same time the word on each thing can tell us something different, because each thing can be in a different place, can be used for a different purpose, or can be seen by different people. In those ways the word on the first thing is not the same as the word on the second thing, because what it tells us can change with the place and the people who look at it. So we have a situation where the word is the same in the sense of the marks and the look, and not the same in the sense of what it makes us think about.

question: Distinguish between using a word and talking about a word. Use 'cat' as your example.
english_query: Distinguish between using a word and talking about a word. Use 'cat' as your example.
ugf_response: First, we look at a name like cat. When we say the cat sits, we are using the name cat to point to a real thing that lives. In that case the name is a tool that lets us talk about the thing and we think about the thing itself. When we instead say the name cat has three sounds, we are not talking about a thing at all. We are talking about the name itself, the group of sounds that we use when we want to point to the thing. In that second case the name is the thing we are looking at, not the thing that lives. The first way we use the name to stand for something else, the second way we look at the name as a thing we can talk about. If we ask someone to show a cat, we want a picture of the thing that lives. If we ask someone to write the name cat, we want the sounds that make the name. This shows how we can either use a name to talk about a thing, or we can talk about the name itself. The two are not the same even though the same set of letters appears.

question: 'Bald' has fuzzy edges. Does that mean some sentences using 'bald' have no clear true-or-false value? Or what?
english_query: 'Bald' has fuzzy edges. Does that mean some sentences using 'bald' have no clear true-or-false value? Or what?
ugf_response: First, we note that a word is like a picture we keep in our mind. Then we see that some words have a picture that is sharp, like red, which is a clear color. Then we see that other words have a picture that is not clear, like bald. Some people think bald means having no hair on the top of the head, but some people think a head that still has a little hair or a short cut can also be called bald. Because the picture is not clear, we can have a line where we are not sure if a case belongs or not. When we make a line of words that says something about a person, we can try to say if it is right or wrong. If the word in the line is one with a not clear picture, then the line may not have a clear right or wrong meaning because we cannot agree on where the line ends. This does not mean the line is broken, only that we need to be careful and maybe add more words to make it clear. So lines that use the word bald can sometimes be hard to say is right or wrong because the word itself does not have a clear line.

question: Distinguish between something being permitted, required, and going above and beyond what is required. Give an example of each.
english_query: Distinguish between something being permitted, required, and going above and beyond what is required. Give an example of each.
ugf_response: First, we see a time when people say you can help people, and that means you are allowed to do it if you want. Then we see a time when people say you have to help people, and that means you must do it, it is something you need to do. Next, we see a time when people say you can also help people more, and that means you do more than you have to, you go past the need and add extra help. This shows the difference between something that is allowed, something that is required, and something that is more than the required amount.

question: 'This sentence is false.' What's the problem? What does the problem teach us about truth or sentences?
english_query: 'This sentence is false.' What's the problem? What does the problem teach us about truth or sentences?
ugf_response: First, we look at the words that say that the words are not true. Then we ask if the words are true, then what they say is correct, which means they are not true, so they can not be true. If the words are not true, then what they say is wrong, which means they are true, so they can not be not true. This makes a back and forth where the words can not be given a clear true or not true label. This shows that some groups of words that talk about themselves can cause a problem for our idea that every statement is either true or not true. It teaches that we have to be careful when a statement tries to tell about its own truth, because it can lead to a back and forth that stops us from deciding what is true. It also shows that the idea that every statement has a single true value may need more rules to keep it working.

question: Could the laws of physics have been different than they are? Why or why not? What hangs on the answer?
english_query: Could the laws of physics have been different than they are? Why or why not? What hangs on the answer?
ugf_response: First, we see that the world has many ways that things move, push, pull, shine, fall and act, and those ways are like rules that tell everything how to behave. Then we ask if those rules could have been not the same as they are now. One way to think about this is that the rules come from the way the world began, so if the beginning had been different, the rules could have been different too. Another way to think about this is that the rules are the only ways that let anything be there at all; if the rules were any other, the world could not hold anything we call life, so they can not be different. This gives us two possible answers: maybe the rules could be different, or maybe they can not be different. Next, we look at why the answer matters. If the rules could be different, then we might be able to find new ways to get power, make new tools, and our thoughts about why things are the way they are would change a lot. If the rules can not be different, then everything we see is fixed by those same rules, and any talk about other possible worlds is just a story, not something we can check or use. So the answer decides whether we think we can ever change the basic ways the world works or whether we must always work inside the same set of ways.

question: If two duties pull you in opposite directions, what do you do? Walk through how to think about a case.
english_query: If two duties pull you in opposite directions, what do you do? Walk through how to think about a case.
ugf_response: First, you see that you have two jobs that both ask you to do something at the same time but they ask different things. Then you think about how each job would feel if you do it or if you do not do it. You can try to see which one matters more for you right now. You can also see if you can do both by changing the time or the way you do them. If you cannot, you have to pick one. You can look at who will be hurt or helped by each choice. You can think about what you can do later to make up for the other thing. In the end you choose the way that feels right for you now.

question: What would it take for a glass to be fragile? Walk through what 'fragile' commits us to.
english_query: What would it take for a glass to be fragile? Walk through what 'fragile' commits us to.
ugf_response: First, we think about a glass as a clear thing that can hold water. Then we ask what would make that thing easy to break. To be easy to break means that a small push, a light drop, or a soft hit can make it crack or fall apart. This means we must treat it as something that can be hurt by little force. For a glass to be easy to break, its shape must be thin, its walls must not be strong, and the stuff it is made of must not hold back a push very well. This also means we need to keep it away from hard things, we need to put it where it will not fall, and we need to pick it up slowly. When we say a glass is fragile, we are also saying that we expect it to break if we are not careful, that we should not put heavy things on it, and that we should not shake it hard. All of these ideas are part of what we think when we call something fragile.

question: 'Snow' has four letters. Snow is white. Why are these two different kinds of claim and what difference does it make?
english_query: 'Snow' has four letters. Snow is white. Why are these two different kinds of claim and what difference does it make?
ugf_response: First, we look at the line that says snow has four letters. This line talks about the letters that make the word snow, not about the thing snow itself. Then we see the line that says snow is white. This line talks about what the thing snow looks like when we see it. The two lines are not the same sort of line because one is about the word and the other is about the thing. This matters because the truth of the first line does not tell us anything about the truth of the second line and the truth of the second line does not tell us anything about the truth of the first line. For example, if you hear that snow is white you might think that it also has four letters, but that is not true; the two ideas are separate and each has to be checked on its own.

question: Can a list of all lists that don't include themselves include itself? Walk through what this asks and why it matters.
english_query: Can a list of all lists that don't include themselves include itself? Walk through what this asks and why it matters.
ugf_response: First, we look at what the question is asking. It talks about a big group that is made of every smaller group that does not have itself inside that group. Then we ask whether that big group has itself inside it. If the big group does have itself, then it does not follow the rule that only groups without themselves can be inside, so it should not be inside the big group. But we just said it is inside, so that makes a problem. If the big group does not have itself, then it follows the rule, so it should be put inside the big group. But we just said it is not inside, so that also makes a problem. In either case we get a conflict, which means the idea of such a big group cannot work in a normal way. This matters because it shows that we cannot just let groups talk about themselves without running into trouble, and we have to be careful when we let things talk about themselves in numbers or in thinking.

question: Why might it be wrong to break a promise even when keeping it leads to a worse outcome overall?
english_query: Why might it be wrong to break a promise even when keeping it leads to a worse outcome overall?
ugf_response: First, we see that a person can give a word that they will do something for another person and that word makes the other person feel safe and trust them. Then, we notice that if the first person does not keep that word, the other person can feel hurt and may not trust anyone later. Even if keeping the word would bring a result that looks bad for many people, breaking the word still hurts the trust that people have in each other. Trust is a small thing that all people need to work together, and when it is broken it can spread and make it hard for people to work together in the future. That future hurt can be bigger than the bad result that might happen now if the word is kept. So, breaking the word can be a bad thing even when keeping it seems to lead to a worse result, because the damage to trust and the feeling of being let down can grow and cause more trouble later.

question: Some thinkers say there are many ways the world could have been, and call these 'possible worlds'. What does this mean and what work does the idea do?
english_query: Some thinkers say there are many ways the world could have been, and call these 'possible worlds'. What does this mean and what work does the idea do?
ugf_response: First, we see that some people who think about big things talk about the fact that there are many ways the world could have been. When they say this, they are not saying that all those ways are real at the same time, but they use the many ways as a way to talk about how things might change if something else had happened. This helps them to see how a change in one part can lead to a different result in another part. By looking at each of the many ways, they can test how a rule or a thought works in each case. This lets them see if the rule works in all the ways or only in some. It also lets them compare the real world to the other ways to see why the real world looks the way it does. In short, talking about many ways the world could have been gives them a tool to think about cause and effect, to check if a thought stays true, and to imagine what would happen if something different had been done.

question: If I had been kinder, things would have gone better. What does 'would have' mean here? How is it different from saying 'they did go better'?
english_query: If I had been kinder, things would have gone better. What does 'would have' mean here? How is it different from saying 'they did go better'?
ugf_response: First, we think about a time that already passed. When we talk about what really happened in that time we use words like did or went, and that tells us the event is a fact. When we want to talk about a time that did not happen but could have happened if something else had been true, we use would have. The words would have work together to show a picture of a possible past, not a real past. So when I say if I had been kinder, things would have gone better, the would have says that the better outcome did not actually happen; it only might have happened if I had been kinder. If I say they did go better, the did says the better outcome really happened. The difference is that would have makes the sentence about an imagined or possible past, while did makes the sentence about a past that actually occurred. The word would is the part that makes it a guess about the past, and have links that guess to the past action. This is why the two sentences do not mean the same thing.

question: Why is there something it's like to taste coffee? Could a perfect description of the brain capture this?
english_query: Why is there something it's like to taste coffee? Could a perfect description of the brain capture this?
ugf_response: First, we notice that when we put a hot drink made from ground seeds in our mouth we get a feeling in our tongue and in our head. This feeling is not just a set of parts moving, it is also the way it feels for the person. It is like a private story that only that person can see inside. Then we think about what a perfect list of everything that is happening in the head would look like. That list could say which parts are active, which tiny bits move, which areas of the head are working. That list would be true about what is happening, but it would not have the private feeling itself. The private feeling is a kind of “what it is like” that comes from the whole of those parts working together, not something you can point to as a separate piece. So even if we had a perfect list of all the parts, we would still need a person to have the feeling to know what it is like. The list can tell us why the feeling happens, but it cannot itself be the feeling. Therefore there is something that it is like to taste the hot drink, and a perfect list of the head does not keep that feeling, because the feeling is the experience, not just the facts about the parts.

question: When someone says 'Bob said "I am hungry"', what's the difference between Bob's word 'I' and the speaker's word 'I'?
english_query: When someone says 'Bob said "I am hungry"', what's the difference between Bob's word 'I' and the speaker's word 'I'?
ugf_response: First, we notice that the word I is a word that tells us who is talking at that moment. Then, when we hear a line that says Bob said “I am hungry”, we have two people in the place. The first person is Bob, the one who actually said the words. For Bob, the word I points back to Bob himself. The second person is the one who tells us about the words. When that person uses the word I, it points back to that person, not to Bob. This means the two I’s are not the same because each one points to a different person. So the meaning of I changes depending on who is doing the talking at that time. When we read or hear the line, we have to keep in mind which person is speaking to know who the I is about.

question: Some words describe themselves (like 'short' is short); some don't (like 'long' is not long). Is 'words that don't describe themselves' a word that describes itself?
english_query: Some words describe themselves (like 'short' is short); some don't (like 'long' is not long). Is 'words that don't describe themselves' a word that describes itself?
ugf_response: First, we look at a word that tells about its own size, such as the word that means “short”. That word has only a few letters, so it really does tell about itself. Next, we look at a word that tells about being “long”. That word also has only a few letters, so it does not tell about its own size. Then we think about the group of all words that do not tell about themselves. The name of that group is made of several words, and those words together have many letters, more than the words inside the group. Because the name says it is a group of words that do not tell about themselves, and the name itself also does not tell about its own size, the name fits the same rule as the other words in the group. This means that the phrase “words that do not tell about themselves” is itself a word (or phrase) that does not tell about itself, so it belongs to the group. In other words, the answer is yes: the description of the group is itself a member of the group, even though that can feel a little odd at first.

question: Imagine someone who knows everything physical about color but has only seen black and white. When they first see red, do they learn something new?
english_query: Imagine someone who knows everything physical about color but has only seen black and white. When they first see red, do they learn something new?
ugf_response: First, we have a person who knows a lot about how light and the thing we call color work in the real world, but this person has only ever looked at black and white. Then, the person hears about a color called red and can think about it, but the mind only has a picture made from words, not from eyes. When the person finally looks at something that is red, the eyes see a new kind of light that is not black and not white. This new sight gives the mind a feeling that was not there before, so the person learns something new about how that color looks. In other words, even though the person already knew the rules of light and color, the first time the eyes see red adds a new piece of knowledge that the mind did not have, and that new piece is the actual look of red. So the answer is yes, the person does learn something new when they first see red.

question: Salt dissolves in water — even when there's no water around. What kind of fact about the salt is this?
english_query: Salt dissolves in water — even when there's no water around. What kind of fact about the salt is this?
ugf_response: First, we look at a piece of salt and we see that if we put it near water it will go into the water and become part of the water. This shows us a fact about the salt that is about what the salt can do, not about how the salt looks. Second, we notice that this fact does not need water to be there right now; it is a statement that tells us how the salt will act when water does appear. Third, because the fact tells us about the way the salt will act, it is a kind of fact that belongs to the salt itself, a fact about the salt’s ability to mix with water. Fourth, this kind of fact does not change the salt; it only describes a way the salt can change when it meets water. So the answer is that the statement “salt dissolves in water” is a fact about the salt’s ability to act with water, a kind of fact that belongs to the salt itself.

question: Some say minds rest on brains in a special way: same brain state, same mind state. Walk through what this claim means and why it is hard to spell out.
english_query: Some say minds rest on brains in a special way: same brain state, same mind state. Walk through what this claim means and why it is hard to spell out.
ugf_response: First, we look at the idea that a person’s mind lives in the part of the body that we call the brain. The say says that if the brain is in one particular form, then the mind is also in one particular form, and that two brains that are in the very same form will have the very same mind. This means we would be able to match each brain form with one mind form. To see why this is hard, we first have to think about what it means to say two brain forms are the very same. The brain is made of many tiny parts that all work at tiny times, and we do not have a way to see every single part at the same moment. Even if we could, we would have to decide which details matter and which do not. Next, we have to think about what it means to say two mind forms are the very same. A mind can be about many things at once, can change in small ways, and we can only talk about it with words that are themselves vague. So we need a clear way to match a brain form to a mind form, and we do not yet have a clear way to do that. In addition, the say says that the way they fit between brain and mind is one‑to‑one, but we can see cases where the same brain form seems to come with different minds, or where different brain forms seem to produce the same mind. This makes it hard to write down a simple guide that always works. Because of these problems with seeing brain forms, seeing mind forms, and checking that they always line up, the idea that the very same brain form always gives the very same mind form is a say that is hard to spell out in a way that everyone can see and try out.

question: Lois believes Superman flies. Lois does not believe Clark flies. But Superman is Clark. How can both her beliefs be coherent?
english_query: Lois believes Superman flies. Lois does not believe Clark flies. But Superman is Clark. How can both her beliefs be coherent?
ugf_response: First, we see that Lois thinks about two names. The first name is Superman. The second name is Clark. When she thinks about Superman, she says that he can fly. When she thinks about Clark, she says that he can not fly. The reason both thoughts can be true at the same time is that she does not know that the two names are for the same person. In her mind the two names are for two different people. If she later learns that the two names are for the same person, she would have to change one of her thoughts. Until then, both thoughts can make sense for her because she treats them as about different people. The fact that one name can be used for the same person does not force her to see them as the same unless she knows that fact. So her two thoughts can stay together without breaking what she thinks is true.

question: Could two people see colors flipped from each other and never know? What follows?
english_query: Could two people see colors flipped from each other and never know? What follows?
ugf_response: First, we think about how each person looks at a thing that gives off light and how that light has a property we call color. When a person looks, the brain makes a feeling that we call a color feeling and the person uses a word that matches the feeling. Now imagine two people, the first person and the second person. The first person’s brain may turn the light that is red into a feeling we call red, while the second person’s brain may turn the same light into a feeling we call green. Both people will still use the same word “red” for the thing because they learned that word from other people. Because they both use the same words, they will not see a difference in what they say or hear. Each will think the other sees the same thing, and because no one can see inside another’s brain, there is no way for them to know that the feelings are swapped. This means that it is possible for two people to have opposite color feelings and never know. The result is that we cannot be sure that any one person’s feeling of a color is the same as anyone else’s feeling of that color. It also means that any claim about what a color feels like cannot be shown by looking at another person, since we have no way to see the feeling inside the other brain. The only way we could tell would be to have some way to look inside the brain, but we do not have that. So the idea leads us to think that we all live in a world where we share words but may have different inner feelings, and that we must accept that we cannot fully know the inner feeling of another. This also means that when we talk about color we are really talking about a shared name, not a shared feeling.

question: One grain of sand isn't a heap. Adding one grain to a non-heap doesn't make a heap. So a heap is impossible. What's wrong with this argument?
english_query: One grain of sand isn't a heap. Adding one grain to a non-heap doesn't make a heap. So a heap is impossible. What's wrong with this argument?
ugf_response: First, we look at the idea that a very small amount of sand is not a big amount, and that adding one more small piece of sand never makes it a big amount. Then we see that the argument says if we start with a small amount that is not a big amount, and we keep adding one piece at a time, we will never get a big amount, so a big amount could never exist. The trouble with this is that the argument uses a rule that says “adding one piece never changes the kind of amount” for every step, but the rule cannot be true for every step. At some point, after many pieces have been added, the total does become a big amount, even though each single addition was just one piece. The mistake is thinking that a small change can never be the change that makes a big difference. In reality, a lot of small changes added together do make the big change, so the rule that “one more never changes it” must stop being true at some point. Because the argument forces the rule to stay true forever, it leads to the wrong result that a big amount is impossible. The error is the hidden assumption that there is a clear line where “not a big amount” turns into “big amount” and that that line can be passed without ever breaking the rule, when in fact the line is vague and the rule must give way at some step. This shows why the original reasoning does not work.

question: Are numbers things in the world? If yes, where? If no, what are they?
english_query: Are numbers things in the world? If yes, where? If no, what are they?
ugf_response: First, we think about what a number is and we see that a number is a way to talk about how many things there are. Then we notice that we can put a number on a piece of paper or on a screen as a group of marks that we can see and touch, and we can also say a number out loud as a sound that other people can hear. This shows that the marks or the sound are things in the world that we can hold or hear, but the idea of the number itself is not a thing you can pick up or see by eye. Next, we understand that the idea of the number lives in the mind or brain of a person, and it can move from one mind to another when people talk or write. After that, we see that the same idea can be shown in many places – on paper, on a screen, in a song, in a talk – and each of those places has a different kind of sign that stands for the same idea. So the answer is that numbers are not things that exist as solid objects in the world; they are ideas that we keep in our heads and that we show by marks, sounds, or other signs that we can see or hear. This is why we can use numbers everywhere even though we cannot hold a number itself.

question: Walk me through what makes something necessarily true versus only sometimes true. Use a concrete example for each.
english_query: Walk me through what makes something necessarily true versus only sometimes true. Use a concrete example for each.
ugf_response: First, we think about what it means for a statement to be true in every case. That means that no matter what the situation is, the words match what is real. For a statement that is always true, we can look at the case of water and wet. The words “water is wet” match the real world for any water you can find, whether it is in a glass, a river, or a cloud. Because there is no water that is not wet, the statement must be true in all cases. Next, we think about a statement that is true only in some cases. That means that sometimes the words match the real world and sometimes they do not. For the case of water and hot, the words “water is hot” are true when the water is heated, such as in a kettle, but they are not true when the water is cold, such as in a lake in winter. Because the statement works in some cases but not in all, it is not a must‑be‑true statement, it is only sometimes true. So the difference is that a must‑be‑true statement works for every possible case, while a sometimes‑true statement works for some cases and fails for others.

question: Can two worlds be exactly alike in all physical ways but different in their moral facts? Why or why not?
english_query: Can two worlds be exactly alike in all physical ways but different in their moral facts? Why or why not?
ugf_response: First, we think about what it means for two worlds to be exactly alike in all the stuff and in the way that stuff moves and works. Then we think about what we mean when we talk about ideas of right and wrong, or what is good or bad, because those are the things people use to tell each other what they think should be done. These ideas are made by the people in the world, and they come from the heads and the feelings that the people have. If the two worlds have exactly the same stuff, the same way the stuff works, and the same heads and feelings for every person, then the ideas about what is good or bad will also be the same, because there is nothing different that could make a person think something else is right or wrong. So the two worlds could not be exactly alike in all physical ways and at the same time have different ideas about what is good or bad. If the ideas about right and wrong were to be different, something about the heads, the feelings, or the way the stuff works would have to be different, and then the worlds would not be exactly alike. Therefore, two worlds that are truly the same in every physical respect must also share the same ideas about what is good and what is bad.

question: Why can't we always swap one true description of a thing for another in a sentence? Walk through a case where the swap fails.
english_query: Why can't we always swap one true description of a thing for another in a sentence? Walk through a case where the swap fails.
ugf_response: First, we look at a short talk that has two parts that both say something real about one thing. One case is that we can say that the dog is big and that the dog can jump high. Both parts are real facts about the dog. If we put them together in a line that says if the dog is big then it can jump high, the line is still real. Now we try to change the first part with the other real fact, so we say if the dog is fast then it can jump high. The new line is not always real because a dog can be fast without being able to jump high. This shows that even though each fact is real by itself, we cannot always change one real fact for another inside the same line and keep the line real. The reason is that the first fact is used as a part that makes the second fact work, and the second fact does not work the same way as that part. So the change fails.

question: If beauty rests on physical features of an object, what does that imply about whether there could be a 'beauty atom' separable from physical features?
english_query: If beauty rests on physical features of an object, what does that imply about whether there could be a 'beauty atom' separable from physical features?
ugf_response: First, we note that when we say something looks nice we are talking about the way its shape, size, color, and other body parts appear to us. Then we see that the nice feeling we have is just the feeling we get when we see those parts together in a certain way. Next, we ask whether there could be a small thing that holds the nice feeling by itself, apart from the shape, size, and color. To answer this we think about what would happen if we took away the shape, size, or color. If we do that, the nice feeling also goes away, because the feeling was only there because of those parts. This means that the nice feeling does not live in a separate small piece that can be taken away from the rest. It is only a result of the parts we see, so there is no extra small thing that can be separated and still make something look nice.

question: Where exactly does someone become tall? Defend your answer or argue there is no exact answer.
english_query: Where exactly does someone become tall? Defend your answer or argue there is no exact answer.
ugf_response: First, we think about what it means to be tall. Then we notice that a person gets taller by growing a little bit at a time. This growth happens inside the body and shows up as a small change in how high the top of the head is from the ground. Because the change is small, there is no single moment that can be pointed to as the one time the person becomes tall. Instead, we can choose a place in the growth line, like the first day the person is higher than a certain number of centimeters, or the day they pass a line that other people call tall. Both ways work, but they are just ways we pick to mark the change. So we can say that there is no one single spot in the body or one single instant in time that makes a person tall; it is a steady change that we can describe by picking a point that we decide is enough to call tall. This shows why we can say there is no single answer, because any point we pick is a choice, not a clear way of the body.

question: Two leaves are both green. What is 'green' — a thing both have, an idea, or what?
english_query: Two leaves are both green. What is 'green' — a thing both have, an idea, or what?
ugf_response: First, we look at two green parts that grow on a plant and we see that they look like each other. Then we notice that we call the way they look “green”. The word green is a word we say and write when we want to tell other people about that look. The word itself is not a thing you can hold, it is not a separate part that sits on the leaf, it is a sign that lives in our heads and in our talk. At the same time it is not only a thought in one mind, because many people can use the same word to talk about the same look. So green is a word that stands for a way of looking that both of the leaves share. It is the word we use to point to the same look on both parts, not a separate object that the parts own.

question: If 'the morning star' and 'the evening star' name the same body in the sky, why is 'the morning star is the evening star' news worth saying?
english_query: If 'the morning star' and 'the evening star' name the same body in the sky, why is 'the morning star is the evening star' news worth saying?
ugf_response: First, we see that people use the words the morning star and the evening star to point to something they see in the sky. Then we notice that the two words are used at different times of the day but they both point to the very same bright thing. This means the two words are like two names for the same thing. When we first learn that the two names match, that is a new fact that changes how we think about the sky and about how names work. Now we can see that the same thing can have two different names, and that can help us see that other things in the world might also have two names we thought were different. Because many people think that different names always mean different things, the news that the morning star is the evening star is worth saying. It shows that a simple change in what we think can happen just by learning that two names are really the same, and that can affect how we talk and learn about other things.
